text.title: semantic-field-naming Core Rules
text.summary: Seven testable rules for naming structured-output fields so the model recruits its pre-trained priors instead of guessing.

rule.statement: Every field name is English, lower_snake_case, and at least 2 tokens. Reject any field named `val`, `flag`, `output`, `result`, `data`, `info`, `field1`, or a single letter.

rationale.source: Instructor 2024-09 case: final_choice → answer raised benchmark accuracy from 4.5% to 95%.

rule.rationale: Generic placeholders force the model to guess what to put there from context alone; specific names recruit pre-trained priors at zero token cost.

rule.statement: Never suffix field names with `_value`, `_data`, `_result`, or `_output` when the rest of the name already carries the meaning. `age_value` → `age_years`; `name_data` → `full_name`; `total_result` → `total_revenue_usd`.
rule.rationale: Redundant suffixes spend tokens, dilute the prior, and add no information that the field type does not already convey.

rule.statement: Numeric fields encode the unit in the name: `age_years`, `monthly_spend_usd`, `weight_kg`, `days_since_last_login`, `confidence_0_to_1`. Currency fields prefer integer minor units (`total_cents`) over float majors (`total_usd`).
rule.rationale: Without the unit token in the name, the model picks an arbitrary unit (cents vs dollars, kg vs lb, 0-1 vs 0-100). Pinning the unit in the name eliminates a whole class of silent hallucinations.

rule.statement: Boolean fields start with `is_`, `has_`, `should_`, or `requires_`. `flag: bool` is forbidden; `is_returning_customer: bool` is mandatory. Avoid double-negatives (`is_not_disabled`).
rule.rationale: Without a direction prefix, `True` is ambiguous (`flag=True` could mean enabled or flagged-as-bad). Direction prefixes are the dominant pattern in training data — the model has seen `is_paid` orders of magnitude more often than `paid_flag`.

rule.statement: Enum-typed strings use semantically-loaded literal vocabularies: `Literal["approve", "reject", "needs_more_info"]`, `Literal["low", "medium", "high", "critical"]`. Reject single-letter or numeric labels (`a|b|c`, `0|1|2`) and reject ambiguous codes (`OK|ERR|NOK`).
rule.rationale: Semantic literal values double as their own documentation; the model maps them to its pre-trained understanding of priority/severity/decision domains without needing a description block.

rule.statement: String fields with format constraints encode the format in the name: `body_markdown`, `title_max_70_chars`, `slug_kebab`, `iso_published_date`, `email_address`, `e164_phone`. Reserve plain `name`, `text`, `value` only for free-form strings with no constraint.
rule.rationale: The format suffix tells the model the parse target up front; otherwise the model emits a freeform string and downstream Pydantic validation has to reject + retry.

rule.statement: Field names match the field's cardinality: scalar fields are singular (`order: Order`), list fields are plural (`orders: list[Order]`), dict-of fields encode the key (`order_by_id: dict[str, Order]`). Never use plural for a scalar (`items: Item`) or singular for a list (`order: list[Order]`).
rule.rationale: The pluralisation prior is extremely strong in training data; mismatched cardinality nudges the model toward emitting the wrong shape (an array when a scalar was expected, or vice versa).
